CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience a nd Society
The History of Vaccination and Current Recommendations

L e arning Objectives
Recognize and describe the contributions of individuals in the advancement of vaccination practices.
01
02
Create a timeline of major events and efforts leading to current vaccination practices.
03
Review the history of modern vaccination recommendations.
04
Reflect on the receipt of vaccines as a child and adult for you and your family.
05
Compare and contrast current vaccine recommendations for children under 6 years old, 7-18 years old, and adults.

Introduction
The history of vaccination goes all the way back to 1000 AD when soars from smallpox survivors were scratched into the skin of others to provide immunity.

Terminology
Inoculation = introduction of material into a person to provoke immunity to a disease
Vaccination = introduction of a material designed to provoke an immune response that will provide protection from a related disease agent
Smallpox = disease caused by the variola virus
Variolation = smallpox immunization technique once practiced in many locations worldwide – transfer of matter from a smallpox sore into a cut in the skin of an uninfected person

Infectious Diseases and Vaccination up to Edward Jenner
Unknown fever struck English royal family, killing Prince Henry at 18
Didn’t know what it was – now know it was Salmonella typhi = typhoid fever
1612
French Jesuits knowingly bring smallpox to North America
Entire villages of Native Americans exterminated to due disease
1625
Smallpox outbreak hits colonial settlers in MA – 20 settlers from Mayflower
Devastated Native American population
John Winthrop, governor, said it was “divine intervention”
1633
Epidemic of yellow fever in Cuba and Yucatan  quarantine was accepted in America
Boston quarantines all ships from West Indies
Outbreak of measles in 1657
1648

Infectious Diseases and Vaccination up to Edward Jenner
Death of Chinese emperor from smallpox – new emperor supported inoculation
Inoculated his children and recommended continuation for all descendants
1661
Queen Mary II died of variola hemorrhagica = internal and pustule bleeding due to smallpox
Age 32
1659
Smallpox epidemic in Boston
Zabdiel Boylston inoculated 248 people, 6 died = 3% fatality rate
Smallpox killed 844 = 14% fatality rate
First variolation in America
1721
Benjamin Franklin lost son to smallpox in 1736
Large outbreak in Charleston, SC in 1738 killed 14% infected and half of Cherokee Indian population
1736 - 1738

Explosion of science and technology beginning mid-1800s lead to a lot of discoveries and advancement in infectious diseases and vaccination
You will continue the story

Introduction to Vaccine Recommendations
Before 1985 – only seven vaccines recommended
Today – children under 2 years get vaccinated for 14 potentially serious diseases
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) makes vaccination schedule based on recommendations from Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) – approved by American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) and American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP)
Who makes recommendations?

Provided to ACIP for Recommendation
The safety and efficacy of the vaccine when given at specific ages.
Only vaccines licensed by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) are recommended, and vaccine makers must conduct rigorous tests during clinical trials to show that a vaccine is safe and effective at specific ages.
The severity of the disease.
Vaccines recommended for children are meant to prevent diseases that can be serious for them, potentially causing long-term health problems, or even death.
How many children get the disease if there is no vaccine.
Vaccines that do not provide benefit to many children may not be recommended.
The differences in how well a vaccine works for children of different ages.
The ability of vaccines to help the body produce immunity can vary depending on the age when the vaccine is given.
